docs.title: Disqualifying And Financial Qualification
docs.description: Rules for disqualifying prospects and verifying financial fit before demo.

headings.title: Has money but is skeptical due to past experiences
Asks hard questions but engages in the process
Shows genuine interest but needs convincing
May say "I need to think about it" but provides logical reasons
To give you an example inside of our agency, a qualified prospect must have:
Brick and mortar location (not home-based or only virtual)
Only anomaly is if they are home-based and do more than $10k+/mo
Full medical spa with multiple treatment options (more than 3+ treatments)
Growth mindset - willing to invest for results
Single treatment focus - only Botox, weight loss, or laser hair removal
Out-of-scope requests - testosterone therapy, general medical
Desperation signals: "I just sold my car to fund this business"
Debt implications: "I'm drowning in debt and need this to work"
Resource limitations: "I only have one cheap device and it cost $3,000"
Survival mode: "If this doesn't work, I'll have to close"
Unrealistic expectations - wants guaranteed results with no risk
Blame mentality - everything is someone else's fault
There are two types of disqualification signals:
ICP Signals - they're not in your ideal customer profile
Financial Signals - they can't afford your services
It all boils down to the perceived value of said thing they’re buying…
When They Say They Don't Have Money:
Note their situation in CRM for future reference
Business situation is worse than indicated
Can we help them? Do they offer any aesthetic treatments?
Will they expand? Are they open to adding aesthetic services?
Referral potential? Do they know medical spa owners?
Opportunity cost? Is this time better spent with qualified prospects?
Provide immediate value - free resources, advice, connections
Stay connected - regular check-ins and market updates
Help them grow - refer them to funding sources, business coaches
Be patient - many successful clients started broke
Identify the specific concern - financial, ICP, urgency, or authority
Determine if it's fixable - can the concern be addressed?
Assess probability - what are the odds they'll close?
Consider opportunity cost - what else could you be doing with this time?
Medium probability + minor concern = Continue with adjusted approach
High value + any concern = Continue (worth the risk)
Financial qualification requires finesse - use soft qualifiers before hard price drops

headings.title: The one job of this call
Core operating frames (read before every shift)
LO positioning bank (weave in naturally — pick what fits)
Pivot back to business (optional — keep it short for investors)
If they ask "Who is this with?" / "What company?"
STAGE 3 — QUICK QUALIFICATION (notes for the LO — NOT underwriting)
Open (they may already answer in Stage 2)
Form callback + dig one level deeper
Follow-up questions (ask only what's missing — max 3–5 total)
Hard disqualifiers (acknowledge, note, escalate — don't promise)
STAGE 4 — TRANSITION TO TRANSFER OR BOOK
Specialization pitch + live transfer (the money line)
If transfer is not possible — book
Pull the appointment closer (every day out drops show rate)
CALL VARIANT A — Laura-booked (AI) appointment confirmation
CALL VARIANT B — Callback after "not a good time"
OBJECTION HANDLING (front-desk version — keep the call alive, hand off)

docs.title: Intro Call Qualification Framework
docs.description: Framework for Pedro (setter) intro calls: qualify, book demo, or route to Boot Camp.

headings.title: Internal Use Only  |  Sales & Setting Team  |  Last Updated: March 2026
By the end of this document, you will understand:
How to apply FUN criteria — softly and only hard-qualifying when necessary
How to identify and capture buying motivators
How to frame the Demo Call so prospects show up instead of no-showing
Every Intro Call must accomplish exactly three things — in this order:
Confirm they meet FUN criteria before investing a Demo slot on them
Identify What Motivates Them to Buy
Capture the specific emotional and personal drivers behind their buying decision
Is their problem one you solve?
Red flag: Keeps asking "How much does it cost?" before understanding the value
Red flag: Asks "Do I have to put money up front?"
Hard Qualification (Only When Soft Cues Fail)
⚠️  Warning — Hard Qualifying Lowers Show Rates
Ready to solve this within weeks
Clearly not ready to act soon
Objective 2 — Identify What Motivates Them to Buy
How to Use It on the Demo
Frame the entire pitch around consistency and system reliability
Cares about caliber, not just volume
Emphasize pre-qualification, education-first ads, and pre-seasoned leads
Wants relief — time and mental load back
Lead with done-for-you, no manual oversight required
Trust is broken — skepticism is high
Flag for closer: objection prevention around proof and credibility is essential
Take notes during the call — write down exact words and phrases they use
Note personal details (daughter, retirement timeline, specific financial goals)
Note emotional emphasis — what do they say with the most energy?
Log it in your notes template and pass it to the Demo closer before the call
⚠️  What Happens If You Skip This
What NOT to Do on the Intro Call
Trying to close or pitch the solution
Going too deep on past attempts or objections
Pitching on command when they ask "what do you do?"
Not framing the next call properly
The Right Mindset for the Intro Call
"I need to figure out if I can actually help them"
"I'll note this and let the Demo closer address it properly"
"Listen more than you talk — what they say matters more than what I say"
"I'll give a confident number and move on — no rabbit holes"
"Get them to the Demo no matter what"
"Only book the Demo if they genuinely qualify"
Confirm Financial fit, Urgency, and Need
Surface what will actually drive their yes
Make them want to show up to the Demo
1. Confirmation the prospect passed FUN criteria
2. Notes on their key motivators and personal drivers
3. A booked Demo slot on their calendar

docs.title: No Shows And Maximizing Show Rates
docs.description: Setter levers to reduce no-shows and increase demo attendance.

headings.title: No Shows & Maximizing Show Rates (Setter Levers)
Why Show Rate Matters For Setters
Why Show Rate Matters For Setters
More shows = more offers = more closes = more commission
Success loves speed, and speed-to-lead is KEY here
When Connecting With Them Over The Phone:
Always double tie down on their time
"So you're confirmed for Tuesday at 2pm, you'll be there right?"
"Perfect, and you'll have your calendar open and be ready to dive in?"
Attempt to push the demo call time forward (within next 24hrs is ideal)
Send sales assets to build trust pre-call

Pre-call video assets (canonical URLs + objection map): Pre-Call Objection Video Assets — use **prospect_page_url** from the index; default sequence 1→2→3 or jump-send per quick-send table; tie-down language in demo confirmation script
Case studies, testimonials, anything that builds credibility
Conduct mini pre-pitch (covered in "Transitioning to Demo" video)
WhatsApp: Text, voice note, video outreach (personalized)
Instagram: Follow them and DM them there
Make sure your IG has case studies and social proof on it
Follow everything covered in Lead Nurture Best Practices
At the end of each day, audit both your and the closer's calendar
Clear out any unqualified appointments or mark them as free
Grade 1-2 apps always marked as free to make space for more qualified prospects
When someone doesn't show up, here's exactly what you do:
Call the number attached to the lead
If no answer: double dial immediately
If no answer to both double dials: send first text
Move them to the no-show pipeline stage
"Hey [NAME], called a few times. Are you interested in rescheduling?"
Once someone no-shows, they automatically move into:
You only move the lead out of no-show if they rebook
